text: ANC Schedule and Checkpoint Reminders: CHW Job Aid

ANC means 'antenatal care' — care given to a woman during pregnancy to keep her and her baby healthy. WHO recommends at least 8 visits during pregnancy. More visits mean better chance of finding problems early. Your job as a CHW is to know when visits should happen, remind women to go, and help them overcome any barriers.
How Many ANC Visits?

WHO recommends 8 antenatal care visits. The first visit should happen as early as possible — before 12 weeks of pregnancy (first 3 months). Every visit matters. Even if a woman misses some visits, she should still go — it is never too late to start ANC.
Visit 1: Before 12 Weeks (First 3 Months)

What happens at Visit 1: (1) Confirm pregnancy and estimate due date. (2) Blood tests: anaemia (low blood), HIV, syphilis, and blood group. (3) Urine test: check for infection and protein (warning sign for high blood pressure). (4) Blood pressure check. (5) Start iron and folic acid tablets. (6) Discuss birth plan, birth location, emergency contacts. (7) Tetanus vaccination if not up to date.
CHW reminder: Help her identify a health facility for birth. Discuss how she will get there. Ask if she has money set aside for transport. Make a plan together.
Visit 2: Around 20 Weeks

What happens at Visit 2: (1) Blood pressure and urine check. (2) Measure the belly to check the baby is growing well. (3) Listen to the baby's heartbeat. (4) Continue iron and folic acid. (5) Check anaemia results and treat if needed. (6) Discuss nutrition — eating enough protein, vegetables, and iron-rich foods.
Visit 3: Around 26 Weeks

What happens at Visit 3: (1) Blood pressure and urine check. (2) Belly measurement. (3) Check baby's position (where the baby is lying). (4) Repeat blood test for anaemia if she was anaemic earlier. (5) Talk about signs of labour and what to do. (6) Remind her of danger signs.
Visit 4: Around 30 Weeks

What happens at Visit 4: (1) Blood pressure and urine. (2) Belly measurement and baby position. (3) Repeat HIV test (second test recommended). (4) Give malaria prevention treatment if in a malaria area (IPTp — ask health worker). (5) Review birth plan and danger signs.
Visit 5: Around 34 Weeks

What happens at Visit 5: (1) Blood pressure and urine. (2) Belly measurement. (3) Check baby's position — the baby should be head down by now. (4) Continue iron and folic acid. (5) Discuss breastfeeding — when to start (within 1 hour of birth), importance of colostrum (first milk). (6) Confirm birth plan is ready.
Visits 6, 7, and 8: 36, 38, and 40 Weeks

Visits become more frequent near the due date. What happens: (1) Blood pressure and urine at every visit. (2) Baby's position and heartbeat. (3) Check for swelling of hands, face, or legs (danger sign for high blood pressure). (4) Check if the baby is head down — if not, the health worker will advise on what to do. (5) Review labour signs and birth plan. (6) If 40 weeks with no labour, the health worker may advise on next steps.
What Women Get at ANC (Each Visit)

Every visit: blood pressure checked, urine tested, belly measured, baby's heartbeat heard, iron and folic acid tablets given, and questions answered. Special tests happen at specific visits — see above. Make sure she keeps her ANC card and brings it to every visit.
How to Support Women to Attend ANC

Common reasons women miss ANC: (1) Do not know it is important — EDUCATE: 'ANC catches problems early and saves lives.' (2) Too far or no transport — PROBLEM-SOLVE: community transport fund, accompany her. (3) Cannot afford it — INFORM: ANC is often free or low cost; check local rules. (4) Husband says no — ENGAGE: offer to talk with the husband or mother-in-law with the woman's agreement. (5) Too busy with other children — HELP: see if another family member can watch the children.

Try to visit every pregnant woman in your community at least once per month. Track who is due for their next visit. A reminder message (even a verbal message through a neighbour) increases attendance.
Danger Signs — Go to the Health Centre NOW

At every visit and every home visit, remind the woman of danger signs that mean 'go to the health centre right away — do not wait for your next ANC appointment': (1) Bleeding from the vagina, (2) Very bad headache, (3) Blurry or spotted vision, (4) Fits or shaking, (5) Fever, (6) Very bad swelling of face or hands, (7) Baby not moving, (8) Water breaking early, (9) Difficulty breathing.
⚠️ These danger signs mean go NOW. Not tomorrow. Not after trying home treatment. GO NOW. Every hour can make the difference between life and death.
